howItWorks.intro: This infill calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Fill Fraction = Shell Fraction + (1 - Shell Fraction) × (Infill% / 100)
Estimated Weight = Volume × Density × Fill Fraction
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What infill percentage should I use?
faq.answer: For decorative items: 5-15%. For functional parts: 20-40%. For structural or load-bearing parts: 50-80%. 100% infill is rarely needed and wastes material.

faq.question: Does infill pattern matter?
faq.answer: Yes. Grid and triangles are strongest. Gyroid provides good strength with flexibility. Honeycomb is popular for a balance of strength and weight.

faq.question: How much material does infill save?
faq.answer: At 20% infill, you save roughly 60-70% of material compared to a solid print, while retaining about 40-50% of the strength.

faq.question: Is the Infill Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard 3D printing and additive manufacturing formulas trusted by makers and 3D printing enthusiasts. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Infill Calculator?
sections.body: The Infill Calculator is a free online tool designed for makers and 3D printing enthusiasts who need quick, accurate calculations in the 3D printing and additive manufacturing space. By entering your part volume, infill percentage, wall thickness, you get instant results including estimated weight, material saved vs solid, relative strength. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting estimated weight right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In 3D printing and additive manufacturing, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by makers and 3D printing enthusiasts worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to optimize your prints with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your part volume and need to find the right estimated weight. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Part Volume (cm³)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your part volume — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: infill percentage, wall thickness, material density. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your estimated weight, material saved vs solid, relative strength. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your part volume before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both estimated weight and material saved vs solid — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to optimize your prints.
If you're unsure about your material density, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
